a|true|college561.txt.passage:
(1) There was a time when big-league university presidents really mattered.
(2) The New York Times covered their every move.
(3) Presidents, the real ones, sought their counsel.
(4) For Woodrow Wilson and Dwight Eisenhower, being head of Princeton and Columbia, respectively, was a stepping-stone to the White House.
(5) Today, though, the job of college president is less and less removed from that of the Avon lady (except the house calls are made to the doorsteps of wealthy alumni).
(6) Ruth Simmons, the newly installed president of Brown University and the first African American to lead an Ivy League school, is a throwback to the crusading campus leaders of old.
(7) She doesn't merely marshal funds; she invests them in the great educational causes of our day.
(8) With the more than $300 million she raised as president of Smith College from 1995 to 2001, Simmons established an engineering program  (the first at any women's school) and added seminars focused on public speaking to purge the ubiquitous" likes"  and" ums"  from the campus idiom.
(9) At a meeting to discuss the future of Smith's math department, one professor timidly requested two more discussion sections for his course.
(10) Her response:" Dream bigger."
(11) Her own dream was born in a sharecropper's shack in East Texas where there was no money for books or toys-she and her 11 siblings each got an apple, an orange and 10 nuts for Christmas.
(12) Though she was called nigger on her walk to school, entering the classroom, she says, " was like waking up."
(13) When Simmons won a scholarship to Dillard University, her high school teachers took up a collection so she'd have a coat.
(14) She went on to Harvard to earn a Ph.
(15) D. in Romance languages.
(16) Simmons has made diversity her No.
(17) 1 campus crusade.
(18) She nearly doubled the enrollment of black freshmen at Smith, largely by traveling to high schools in the nation's poorest ZIP codes to recruit.
(19) Concerned with the lives of minority students once they arrive at school, she has fought to ease the racial standoffs that plague so many campuses.
(20) At Smith she turned down a request by students to have race-specific dorms.
(21) In 1993, while vice provost at Princeton, she wrote a now famous report recommending that the university establish an office of conflict resolution to defuse racial misunderstandings before they boiled over.
(22) Her first task at Brown will be to heal one such rupture last spring after the student paper published an incendiary ad by conservative polemicist David Horowitz arguing that blacks economically benefited from slavery."
(23) There's no safe ground for anybody in race relations, but campuses, unlike any other institution in our society, provide the opportunity to cross racial lines,"  says Simmons. "
(24) And even if you're hurt, you can't walk away.
(25) You have to walk over that line."

(20) Their light-if they give much light-is too small for us to see.

a|true|college566.txt.passage:
(1) As long as her parents can remember, 13-year-old Katie Hart has been talking about going to college.
(2) Her mother, Tally, a financial-aid officer at an Ohio university, knows all too well the daunting calculus of paying for a college education.
(3) Last year the average yearly tuition at a private, four-year school climbed 5.
(4) 5 percent to more than $17,000.
(5) The Harts have started saving, and figure they can afford a public university without a problem.
(6) But what if Katie applies to Princeton  (she's threatening), where one year's tuition, room and board-almost $34,000 in 2002-will cost more than some luxury cars?
(7) Even a number cruncher like Tally admits it's a little scary, especially since she'll retire and Katie will go to college at around the same time.
(8) Paying for college has always been a humbling endeavor.
(9) The good news: last year students collected $74 billion in financial aid, the most ever.
(10) Most families pay less than full freight.
(11) Sixty percent of public-university students and three quarters of those at private colleges receive some form of financial aid-mostly, these days, in the form of loans.
(12) But those numbers are not as encouraging as they appear for lower-income families, because schools are changing their formulas for distributing aid.
(13) Eager to boost their magazine rankings, which are based in part on the test scores of entering freshmen, they're throwing more aid at smarter kids-whether they need it or not.
(14) The best way to prepare is to start saving early.
(15) A new law passed last year makes that easier for some families.
(16) So-called 529 plans allow parents to sock away funds in federal-tax-free-investment accounts, as long as the money is used for" qualified education expenses"  like tuition, room and board.
(17) The plans aren't for everyone.
(18) For tax reasons, some lower-and middle-income families may be better off choosing other investments.
(19) But saving is vital.
(20) When's the best time to start?"
(21) Sometime,"  says Jack Joyce of the College Board, " between the maternity ward and middle school."
(22) Aid packages usually come in some combination of grants, loans and jobs.
(23) These days 60 percent of all aid comes in the form of low-interest loans.
(24) All students are eligible for" unsubsidized"  federal Stafford loans, which let them defer interest payments until after graduation.
(25) Students who can demonstrate need can also qualify for federal Perkins loans or" subsidized"  Staffords, where the government pays the interest during school.
(26) Fortunately, this is a borrower's market. "
(27) Interest rates are at their lowest level in the history of student loans,"  says Mark Kantrowitz, publisher of Finaid.
(28) Kantrowitz expects rates to fall even further when they're reviewed this summer.
(29) Traditional scholarships, academic or athletic, are still a part of many families' planning.
(30) Mack Reiter, a 17-year-old national wrestling champion, gets so many recruiting letters he throws most away.
(31) He'll almost certainly get a free ride.
(32) Without it, " we would really be in a bind,"  says his mother, Janet.
(33) For everyone else, it's worth the effort to pick through local and national scholarship offerings, which can be found on Web sites like collegeboard.
(34) com.

a|notTrue|college1162.txt.passage:
(1) The poverty line is the minimum income that people need for an acceptable standard of living.
(2) People with incomes below the poverty line are considered poor.
(3) Economists study the causes of poverty in order to find solutions to the problem.
(4) As the general standard of living in the country rises, the poverty line does, too.
(5) Therefore, even with today's relatively high standard of living, about 10 percent of the people in the United States are below the poverty line.
(6) However, if these people had stable jobs, they could have an acceptable standard of living.
(7) Economists suggest several reasons why poor people do not have jobs.
(8) For one thing, more than half of the poor people in the United States are not qualified to work.
(9) Over 40 percent of the poor people are children.
(10) By law, children less than 16 years old cannot work in many industries.
(11) A large number of poor people are old.
(12) Many companies do not hire people over 65 years old, the normal retirement age.
(13) Some poor adults do not look for jobs for a variety of personal reasons: they are sick, they do not have any motivation.
(14) they have family problems, or they do notbelieve that they can find a job.
(15) Other poor people look for a job but cannot find one.
(16) Many poor adults never went to high school.
(17) Therefore, when they look for jobs, they have few skills that they can offer.
(18) At the present time, the government thinks it can reduce poverty in the country in the following ways.
(19) First, if the national economy grows, businesses and industries hire more workers.
(20) Some of the poor who are qualified to look for jobs may find employment.
(21) Then they will no longer be below the poverty line.
(22) Second, if society invests in the poor, the poor will become more productive.
(23) If the government spends money on social programs, education, and training for poor people, the poor will have the skills to offer.
(24) Then it is more likely that they can find jobs.
(25) Finally, if the government distributes society's income differently, it raises some poor people above the poverty line.
(26) The government collects taxes from the non-poor and gives money to the poor.
(27) These payments to the poor are called welfare.
(28) In 1975 over 18 million people in the United States received welfare.
(29) Some economists are looking for better solutions to the poverty problem.
(30) However, at the present time, many people depend on welfare for a minimally acceptable standard of living.

(1) In a lab in Princeton University's ultra-sleek chemistry building, researchers toil in a modern-day hunt for an elusive power: alchemy.
(2) Throughout the centuries, alchemists tried in vain to transform common metals like iron and lead into precious ones like gold or platinum.
(3) Today, Paul Chirik, a professor of chemistry at Princeton, has managed a new twist on the timeworn pursuit.
(4) Dr. Chirik, 39, has learned how to make iron function like platinum, in chemical reactions that are crucial to manufacturing scores of basic materials.
(5) While he can't, sadly, transmute a lump of iron ore into a pile of valuable jewelry, his version of alchemy is far more practical, and the implications are wide-ranging.
(6) The process could herald a new era of flexible manufacturing technologies, while enabling companies to steer clear of scarce elements as prices rise or obtaining them becomes environmentally or geopolitically risky."
(7) No chemist would think lithium was in short supply,"  Dr. Chirik said, " but what happens if you put a lithium battery in every car?
(8) This is why chemistry needs to be ahead of the curve.
(9) We need to have adaptable solutions."
(10) Despite the cost and relative scarcity of precious metals-iridium, platinum, rhodium-we rely on them to manufacture products from denim to beer, pharmaceuticals to fuel cells.
(11) The elements are used as catalysts, substances that kick off or enable chemical reactions.
(12) Dr. Chirik's work involves dissolved catalysts, which are mixed into the end product.
(13) The molecules of the catalyst dissipate during the reaction.
(14) For instance, a solution containing platinum is used to make silicone emulsifiers, compounds that in turn feed products like makeup, cookware and glue.
(15) Tiny amounts of the expensive metal are scattered in all these things; your jeans, for instance, contain unrecoverable particles of platinum.
